coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a significant component of fluorescence nanoparticles with exceptional chemical properties including excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and chemical inertness. CDs also have good optical properties such as broad excitation spectra, tunable emission spectra, and high photo-stability, drawing a lot of attention. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the two main subgroups of CDs. This review article presents recent developments in CD-based bioimaging and highlights critical surface modification techniques including doping, surface functionalization, polymer capping, nano-composite, and core–shell structures to improve their cutting-edge applications. We also outline recent developments in the field of CD-based bioimaging and summarize accomplishments in two broad categories: in vivo applications focusing on CD distribution and uptake for imaging-guided drug delivery and tumor therapeutics.

head.ce:title: Engineering and surface modification of carbon quantum dots for cancer bioimaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a significant component of fluorescence nanoparticles with exceptional chemical properties including excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and chemical inertness. CDs also have good optical properties such as broad excitation spectra, tunable emission spectra, and high photo-stability, drawing a lot of attention. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the two main subgroups of CDs. This review article presents recent developments in CD-based bioimaging and highlights critical surface modification techniques including doping, surface functionalization, polymer capping, nano-composite, and core–shell structures to improve their cutting-edge applications. We also outline recent developments in the field of CD-based bioimaging and summarize accomplishments in two broad categories: in vivo applications focusing on CD distribution and uptake for imaging-guided drug delivery and tumor therapeutics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the most promising approaches to cancer detection, optical bioimaging with fluorescent materials has attracted much interest. Optical imaging has many benefits not offered by other imaging modalities derived from its high spatial and temporal resolution [1]. Nowadays, fluorescent semiconductor nanostructures (quantum dots, QDs) open a new avenue for optical imaging due to their significant advantages such as small sizes (less than 10 nm), high photo-stability, high quantum yield, non-blinking fluorescence, and tunable color. However, the cytotoxicity effect of quantum dots severely limits their in vivo applications [2]. Therefore, the searches for benign alternatives have continued. Also, synthesizing biocompatible fluorescent agents for early-stage cancer imaging is challenging. Carbon nanotubes' unique optical properties and biocompatibility make them good bioimaging contrast agents. Nowadays, the most prominent imaging method uses Carbon nanotube optical properties. Carbon nanotubes' optical properties make them suitable for bioimaging. Photoacoustic bioimaging uses carbon nanotube's strong NIR-I window adsorption. Studies have shown that carbon nanotubes can be employed as a contrast agent in the photoacoustic imaging of a mouse tumor model, encouraging imaging and diagnosis studies [3,4].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also known as the carbon nano-dot, are an emerging indispensable nanomaterial and represent a new type of fluorescent agent for cancer detection. CQDs are defined as a new type of carbonaceous nanostructures and their outstanding features of those, such as low toxicity, excellent water dispersibility, photo-stability, flexibility in surface modification, high biocompatibility, good optical properties, photoluminescence emission, easier of synthesizing at large-scale, low cost, are comparable to quantum dots [5]. Unique features enable CQDs to have been applicable in optical sensing, bioimaging, and optical monitoring. Fluorescent-based CDs are of two types: carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Importantly, numerous methods with so many different sources are developed for CQD synthesis after only one decade, which could be broadly classified into two types of synthesis strategies ''bottom-up“ and ''top-down.” Synthesis methods, classes, sources, and other properties of CQDs are reviewed in the literature [6,7].

Cytotoxicity of CDs we evaluated in a lot of studies. For example in one study researchers examined the toxicity of CDs with oligomeric PEG-passivated surfacesin vitroandin vivo. Incubating CDs with HT-29 and MCF7 cells examined their impact on cell survival, morality, and proliferation. The result showed that CDs did not toxic effect on the cells. They tested CDs' intravenous toxicity in mice. Even after a long exposure time, no significant toxicity was observed by CDs at the concentration required for bioimaging [8]. PPEI-EI, BPEI, PEI, and PAA-functionalized CDs were tested for cytotoxicity. Compared to raw PPEI-EI, PPEI-EI functionalized CDs were harmless, whereas PEI-functionalized CDs were substantially more harmful to HT29 cells. This can be resultant of PEI having more ethyleneimine units than PPEI-EI. Free PAA in aqueous solutions damaged cells even at low doses. PAA-functionalized CDs have the same cytotoxicity as free PAA. Thus, CDs functionalized with low-cytotoxic chemicals are suitable for biological applications even at higher doses. Chen and colleagues also examined the injection methods' effect on biodistribution, clearance, and tumor uptake. They tagged near-infrared dye ZW800 with PEG-functionalized CDs to study biodistribution. To assess biodistribution, they delivered CDs intravenously, subcutaneously, and intramuscularly (im). For all three instances, large amounts of CDs were collected in the kidneys within one hour after injection. Depending on the injection methods, the body clears CD iv > im > sc, respectively. They also found that sc > iv > im injections had varied tumor uptake capabilities. In conclusion, adequately functionalized CDs are biocompatible and valuable in biological applications [9,10].

Thanks to their properties, CQDs have long been considered promising for cancer imaging. These dots have successfully been used for in vitro cell and tissue imaging with one- and two-photon excitations (reviewed in ref 4). However, since CQDs absorption wavelength is in Ultra-Violet (UV) region, the use of those for in vivo imaging is limited. This fundamental challenge has become a hot topic in chemistry and many related fields. Aims of overcoming this challenge, diverse engineered strategies have been exanimated to convert the CQDs into promising candidates for imaging and diagnosis especially target-specific imaging. Several review articles [11–14] on the synthesis and applications of CQDs in nanomedicine have been written. Besides, this CDs can be used for the detection of drugs. Alvandi et al. synthesized carbon dots that can detect the pesticides confidor, dialen super, and, duplosan. The prepared nanosensor does not identify the pesticides bromoxynil, diazinon, glyphosate, malathion, and deltamethrin, confirming its detection specificity [15,16]. This review will discuss the engineering of CQDs to design the fluorescent in vivo imaging probe. For this purpose, we will focus on the recent engineering progress for applying CQDs to targeted specific bioimaging. And finally, some perspectives, challenges, and opportunities to reach this purpose will be discussed.

Modification is one of the most crucial techniques for improving CDs for biological applications. The engineering of CDs will lessen some downsides such as dose-dependent toxicity and photo-bleaching. Additionally, biological aspects like cell transfection and qualities like photoluminescence (PL) are improved. Doping, as well as surface functionalization, polymer capping, and composite, and core–shell structures are standard modification methods of CQDs. This section will explain these methods.

Doping with chemical elements is one method for altering CDs' optical properties. Zhang et al. suggested that doping of larger particles' surface and size effects led to a higher redshift when doped than smaller particles [17]. Nitrogen (N) was initially incorporated into the GQD framework to generate N-GQDs [18]. Due to the emission location of the sp2 cluster and consistent size, NGQDs have independent PL properties [19]. N-GQDs can be produced by hydrothermally processing graphene oxide (GO) in of ammonia solution without any modification [20]. The artificial N-GQDs had a significant blue brightness with a maximum quantum yield (QY) of 24.6 %. Nitrogen also was added to the structure of GQDs via plasma pulsed laser ablation (PLA) resulting in a blue fluorescence [21]. Ju et al. [22] examined how the N concentration affected photoluminescence characteristics. Indeed, as the N atoms in GQDs have a strong attraction for electrons, so, the addition of N causes the emission wavelength to shift toward blue because. The photoluminescence intensity increases 25 times more than in GQDs at a dopant concentration of 16.7 M. Ren and coworkers with the use of biomass produced a new type of N-doped microporous CQDs (NM-CQDs) possessing dual-wavelength photoluminescence emission and excellent QY. Photoluminescence with multi emissions wavelength was observed in the indigo-blue range. This explanation reveals that excited electrons could be transferred from the intrinsic to the surface orbital. NM-CQDs had a QY of 32.4 %, which was much greater than the QY of free CDs [23]. Karadag et al. have proposed a novel strategy for the fabrication of boron (B) and nitrogen (N)–co-doped CQDs (B/N-CQDs) by a simple yet facile hydrothermal method. For this, dopamine and sodium borohydride were employed as C/N and B sources, respectively. CQDs were synthesized with excitation-dependent emission nature and high QY. The B/N-CQDs exhibited low toxicity and good biocompatibility through the cell viability tests by using MRC5 and A549 cell lines with IC50 values of 981 and 583 ppm, respectively. Lastly, the incubation of B/N-CQDs with an A549 cell line led to the emergence of blue and green colors under an excitation wavelength of 405 and 488 nm, respectively, indicating the excitation-dependent nature [24].

Further evaluations show that photoluminescence emissions of NM-CQDs are independent of irradiation time, pH, temperature, and excitation wavelength. These features together make the material extremely promising for biological imaging. In a study, copper atoms were doped into CDs (Cu-CDs) to image H2S in living cells. The lung cancer H3122 line was used as a model to test the viability of Cu-CDs treatment of 100 M NaHS results in a sizable intracellular photoluminescence quenching. NaHS was then re-added which significantly reduced the intracellular photoluminescence. The findings showed that in their produced form Cu-CDs had significant potential for dynamic monitoring of cellular H2S level distributions and fluctuations [25]. In addition, Jin et al. [26], reported that high-quality cell imaging was carried out using sulfur-doped GQDs (S-GQDs). S-GQDs have a moderate level of cytotoxicity and can penetrate cell membranes and when exposed to cytoplasm, they did not change cells morphologically. More results have shown that an increase in the concentration of S-GQDs decreased cell viability. Similar to N and S, the doping of other elements such as boron (B), phosphorus (P), chlorine (Cl), and fluorine (F), have been utilized for bioimaging [27–29]. Feng et al. [30] investigated the impact of B doping on the optical properties of GQDs. With the BC3 bonding configuration, it was feasible to observe the red-shift in the absorbed wavelength of the B-doped GQD (C40B2H18). Additionally, when GQD-B2 made covalent bonds with BC2O or BCO2, a blue shift was seen compared to GQD-B2. Both surface and edge doping brought on these modifications in the absorption peaks. Co-doped GQDs or CQDs, which are materials with heteroatoms such as F-N [31], NP [32], N-Cl [33], and others mixed, have also been studied by some research groups.

This sort of doping offered opportunities to alter the optical, electrical, and structural properties. For instance, co-doping changed GQDs' electrochemiluminescence (ECL). According to research by Zhang et al., S and N doped GQDs (sN-GQDs) with K2S2O8 had an ECL that was 5.8 times higher than the ECL of the GQDs. In another study, Kundu et al. [34] invented the new single-step synthesis of S, N, and F co-doped GQDs by subjecting carbon nanotubes (CNTs) to multiwalled (MW) treatment. They can successfully reach the high QY. It was suggested that the interaction between defect clusters and dopants may be the reason for this high QY. Nafiujjaman et al. [33] generated chlorine (Cl) and N-doped GQDs (Cl-GQDs-N) with a size of 30 nm utilizing a straightforward hydrothermal method. The photoluminescence intensity increased noticeably when N and Cl were introduced to GQDs. Under UV irradiation, Cl-GQDs-N demonstrated strong blue photoluminescence emission with a PL QY of 20.39 %. Cl-GQDs-excitation-dependent when N's emission was investigated, it was found that as the wavelength increased, the emission peaks were more robust. There is currently discussion about the excitation-dependent emission profile's mechanism. In vitro cell uptake of Cl-GQDs-N's indicated that it diffused into the cells satisfactorily, posing the possibility of a band gap transition or a particular energy trap structure being responsible [35]. Moreover, the cytotoxicity assay of Cl-GQDs-N in high doses on canine kidney and cancer cells confirmed their biocompatibility for bioimaging utilization.

Species with reaction sites on their surfaces can be used to functionalize GQDs and CQDs that among them amino modifications, which in particular attracted a lot of attention [36]. Functional species allow for the tuning of optical characteristics and the alteration of the band gap. Precision targeting is possible with the functionalization of NPs with antibodies, small molecules, peptides, aptamers, etc [37].. These moieties increase NP concentration in tumor site and preventing off-target healthy tissues and organ accumulations The primary functionalization technique for targeted imaging is providing non-covalent and covalent interactions between CDs and overexpressed tumor cell surface markers [35,38]. For example, the amine groups of the cysteine interact with the carboxylate groups of GQDs result the production of cysteine-functionalized GQDs (cys-GQDs) [39]. The UV–vis spectrum shows that cys-GQDs have redshifts compared to free GQDs. Additionally, the redshift caused by 365 nm laser excitation may be noticed by increasing the cysteine content. Due to the electron-donating amine groups of cysteine on their surface, the produced GQDs showed a higher QY of around 28 % compared to the QY of about 8.8 % for the naked GQDs [39].

According to Wang et al. [40], several enhancements in optical characteristics were discovered during the functionalization of GQDs. They asserted that the QY of GQDs grew from 4.7 % to 13.8 %. Additionally, functionalized GQDs showed a fivefold increase in photoluminescence intensity over clean GQDs. Fu et al. [41] altered the copper ion-sensitive CDs by using thiosemicarbazide (TSC). By conjugating TSC on the CDs via amide bonds, they were able to create NPs with an approximate size of 2.38 nm. UV–vis analysis of the NPs revealed their optical properties, and conjugation led to the emergence of a new peak at 289 nm. The stability of the photoluminescence of CDs in a pH range (from 3 to 10) was another advantage of functionalization, which was made possible by the high pKa value of the TSC and its undisturbed state. Additionally, TSC-CQDs remained stable in solutions with various NaCl concentrations. GQDs functionalization also alters their hydrophobicity and optical characteristics. For instance, in the study, by Deka et al. [42], GQDs were functionalized with dodecyl amine and then reduced with glycine. Covalent connections between GQDs and hydrophobic long-chain alkyl groups (C12H27) were formed. They were essential components of hydrophobicity and came about due to dodecyl modification. The oxygen functional groups formed green photoluminescence on the surface of GQDs. These groups contracted after functionalization, and blue photoluminescence was seen.

Aptamers are one of the essential tools for NP modification and targeting. Liu et al. [43] functionalized CDs for tumor targeting using an anti-VEGF aptamer. The NH2-PEG-NH2 was then attached to the surface of the CDs as a result of an interaction between the PEG and CDs. The reaction between the carboxyl group and the amine group led to the deposition of the aptamers on the PEG-coated CDs. In comparison with control group, aptamer-conjugated CDs increased the photoluminescence of cells Furthermore, since CDs are positively charged, and mitochondrial membranes are negatively charged, CDs choose mitochondria as their target [43]. Motaghi et al. [44] described a novel method for identifying cancer cells that involves changing CDs using the AS1411 aptamer. This approach relied on cell-surface nucleolin. CDs without an aptamer lacked particular targeting because they dampened the photoluminescence intensity of both fibroblast and cancer cells. However, due to variances in nucleolin expression on cancer cells, aptamer-attached CDs had variable PL intensities.

Folic acid (FA) is a separate chemical substrate that can be used to modify the CD surfaces and its interaction with folate receptors causing the attachment to the surface of cancer cells [45]. Mewada et al. [46] functionalized the surface of CDs for drug delivery by employing FA as a targeted molecule and bovine serum albumin (BSA) to improve the CDs' biocompatibility and capability for drug loading. The BSA was surface-coated through interactions between the functional groups of BSA and the carboxyl group of CDs. The systemic toxicity for healthy cells is reduced by using the FA as a targeting agent. Cancer diagnosis employed the FA@CDs. Zhao et al. employed CDs from ethane diamine as their resource [47]. Because of this, CDs' surfaces had amino groups to connect covalently with FA's carboxyl group. More evaluation has shown that the folate receptor, primarily present on cell membranes, is responsible for the functionalized CDs' ability to adsorb. According to Hai et al., folic acid-encapsulated GQDs can image cancer cells. They provide pH sensing probe and cell imaging nanostructures that are successfully used for detection in cell culture by utilizing FA@GQDs. In this study, FA@GQDs HeLa cells were subjected to different pH treatments to analyze cell FL imaging. The results showed that as the pH increased to 8, the green channel of photoluminescence intensity in HeLa cells brightened, but the blue channel virtually stayed steady. GQDs, as was already mentioned, only exhibit variations in the photoluminescence emissions intensity rather than their excitation wavelength [48].

Numerous imperfections on the surface of CDs make them vulnerable to non-radiative recombination, which decreases PL QY [49]. This challenge could be alleviated through surface passivation procedures such as capping the CDs with polymer chains. Additionally, this modification also improves the optical qualities [50]. Sun et al. [51] employed di-amine terminated oligomeric poly-(ethylene glycol) for surface passivation. Acid-treated particles were mixed with PEG, and the mixture was heated for 72 h at 120C. Surface energy traps were diminished due to surface passivation's effectiveness. As a result, the QY ranged from 4 % to 10 %. Gonçalves et al. [52] produced CDs by laser ablation and then, functionalized them with PEG and mercaptosuccinic acid. Results have shown while modified CDs’ surface flaws grow with longer reaction times, PL intensity decreases.

In the context of biomedical applications, researchers are concerned about these materials' cytotoxicity and environmental danger [51]. Addressing this limitation, Chandra et al. [53] have shown that PEG matrix-encased GQDs lessened their toxicity. They produced GQDs (C-GQDs) obtained from MWCNT, PEGylated them to produce P-GQDs, and then used a hydrothermal method to produce H-GQDs as a control sample. Fig. 1
a, b depicts how each treatment affected the viability of the cells. PEGylation significantly decreased GQD toxicity in high doses. Fig. 1 c-f indicate that ROS was reduced and drug transport capacity was raised due to PEGylation, respectively. Chong et al. [54] coated CQDs with PEG and subjected them to a cresyl violet treatment for in vivo tumor imaging. These CQDs offer the potential for bioimaging because they are benign.

Similar to PEG, polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is another polymer that can be used as a passivation agent. Sachdev et al. [55] selected polyethylenimine (PEI) as a passivation agent and contrasted it to PEG. The chitosan as the original precursor was the similar for both batches, but CD-PL PEI's qualities of high QY, more lifetime, and high PL intensity were superior to CD-PEG's. CD-PEI also boosted A549 and BHK-21 cells' vitality, and the difference between the two increases noticeably at more concentrations. In another study, Gao and coworkers[56] showed that GQDs modified with PEI of different MW could exhibit a range of PL emissions. PEI1800 GQDs, PEI600 GQDs, and Bare GQDs were the types of GQDs employed in this investigation. After an 18-hour incubation period, U-87 cells were dyed yellow, blue, and red to show that they were linked to GQDs, PEI1800GQDs, and PEI600GQDs. Havrdova et al. [57] investigated the impact of surface charge on toxicity using PEI, virgin, and PEG coatings. Results of the study showed that positively charged PEI was the most lethal coating, capable of entering the cell nucleus. On the other hand, neutral PEG eventually had almost no degenerative effects on cells, while negatively charged pristine created specific aberrations due to its interaction with cells.

The interaction of carbon-based NPs with other nanomaterials may also change or add some of their original characteristics [58]. Numerous research [59,60] have coupled nanomaterials made of carbon with metal–organic frameworks (MOFs). Due to their superior optical and electrical qualities, MOFs, CDs, and GQDs can be used in various applications, including photo-catalysis, energy storage, and sensing [61]. MOFs also have a high surface area and microporosity. Designed by Wang et al. [62], CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. It was possible to see the CDs@ZIF-8 shape, which was a typical diamond dodecahedral unit with porosity and intense orange-red PL emission. This emission demonstrated the potency of the CDs@ZIF-8 coating. In one study, fluorescent composite contin of CDs were prepared with l-cysteine, treated with insulin, and decorated with PEG and methylcellulose for oral administration. As a result, these developed CDs were administered orally to diabetic animals and their blood sugars were reduced by about 60 % within 5 h [63]. Su et al. [64] employed mesoporous silica NPs (MSNs) covered with CQDs to make the ECL detecting system for detecting MCF-7 cancer cells. In vitro FL imaging of cancer cells on the modified indium tin oxide electrode before and after 8 h of CQDs@MSNs incubation in the cell culture. Numerous factors affect the intensity of the ECL. For instance, the length of the incubation period directly influences ECL intensity. Therefore, the more significant the reaction, the longer the incubation period. After 50 min of incubation, the ECL response intensified progressively. The optimal ratio of the aptamers used to make the nanoprobes, CQDs@MSNs to MUC1, is presented in addition to the incubation duration. In addition to superior ECL performance, these nanocomposites also had large surface areas, porous architectures, and more active sites, allowing electrons to move freely inside the structure and immobilize the CQD surface.

Additionally, 3D GR@AuNPs are used as a strong film with the properties of biocompatibility and conductivity. Therefore, 4:1 is the ideal CQDs@MSNs to MUC1 aptamer ratio for the production of ECL nanoprobes. It was also shown that the ECL curve altered when the MCF-7 cells density grew. The same scenario was examined for SKBr-3 or K562 cancer cell detection, and it was found that the background and the weak ECL signal were identical. MCF-7 cancer cells, on the other hand, were significantly more straightforward to find than SKBr-3 or K562 cells [64,65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs for cancer imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Developed CDs with unique qualities such as the ability to alter surface functionalities, exceptional photo-stability, high brightness, considerable biocompatibility, and spontaneous penetration capacities, make them highly appealing probes for imaging-guided biomedical applications [71–74]. Different cell or tissue types exhibit distinct responses to foreign NPs depending on specific shapes and structures and related biomarkers on the membrane or in the cytoplasm [75 76]. Functionalization of CDs specific membrane lipids, proteins, targeting ligands, and biomarkers from various cell types enabled them for practical in vivo and in vitro imaging applications as discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs for in vitro cancer cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Cancer devours the lives of millions of people every year and is like ghosts preoccupied with human health. Therefore, early cancer diagnosis via improved techniques is an increasingly important requirement to stop the growth of tumors. Different cancer cells can be penetrated by specifically functionalized CDs, which can be more effectively probed by fluorescent (FL) imaging [77–80]. The distinctive identification methods are driven by the interaction between active CDs and the cancer cells' targeted area. According to research by Ruan et al. [81], glutamic acid and glucose were precursors to making PEGylated CDs. By identifying the angiopep-2 surface group, the-prepared CDs could preferentially anchor glioma cells, increasing the sensitivity of glioma imaging compared to normal brain tissues. Breast cancer cells successfully ingested the yellow emission CDs by endocytosis [82]. Wang et al. [79], developed nitrogen- and chloride-doped CDs possessing multicolor imaging potentials by detecting cytochrome c and intracellular pH in HeLa cells. Recently, more environmentally friendly CDs were created using biomass from natural sources with less environmental impact. These CDs were then used in cancer cell imaging to reduce costs and simplify the synthesis process. For example, Liang et al. [83] developed a new type of carbon dots for photodynamic therapy (PDT) of mice tumors and cancer cells directed by near-infrared fluorescence imaging. Additionally, Ramanan et al. [84], made PL CDs from eutrophic algal blooms that served as a biomarker in imaging the MCF-7 cell line. These findings showed a new potential for cancer cell identification in fluorescent CDs in biomedical studies. Besides this, CDs are promising nanocarriers for drug delivery, theranostic, and stem cell tracing, and deciding the fate of stem cell differentiation [85–87].

The CDs' ability to target cancer cells, has been specifically improved through employing sensitive aptamers. For example, the Salimi group's [88], reported that the hybridization of ultrasensitive antibody-ssDNA (single strand DNA) aptamer sandwich type fluorometric immunosensor with CDs results in generation of the CD-antibody hybrid as the probe for selective imaging of the cancer cells. Additionally, N-doped CDs with a mimic antibody layer were created to be highly specific and particularly identify tumor biomarkers [89]. While potent, however, continues to be the precise labeling of cancer cells with CDs because not all cancer cells have the same recognition molecules and ligands on their surfaces. For instance, folic acid conjugated CDs were employed to image cancer cells. However, the folate receptor was present in other cancer cells besides hepatoma and breast cancer. Therefore, enhancing fluorescent CD targeting and specificity is crucial for tracking cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs for in vivo cancer imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In vivo imaging applications based on FL CDs for clinical diagnosis and therapy based on in vitro investigations have recently benefited from many projects [90 91,92]. By using three injection routes in mice, Yang et al. reported the in vivo imaging of CDs for the first time [93]. Following that, a growing number of meticulous investigations have been carried out to investigate how animal models use CDs in vivo. This section includes a summary of the biodistribution, clearance, and uptake of CDs, their use as imaging probes for brain tissue and brain tumors, and cancer theranostics led by red fluorescence imaging.

Even while CDs are essential for contemporary biomedical research, there is a chance that they could be harmful over time, have poor stability, or accumulate unintentionally in organs and tissues. Therefore, assessing CDs' in vivo biodistribution and uptake is crucial. Tao et al. used the radio-labeling technique to evaluate the biodistribution of CDs. They conducted the first systematic examination of near-infrared (NIR) FL CD performance under in vivo settings. The findings demonstrated that CDs in mice initially accumulated extensively in the kidneys and reticuloendothelial system (RES) before being gradually eliminated through the renal and fecal channels with minimal harmful consequences [94]. To achieve mitochondria-targeted cellular imaging and distinguish malignant cells from normal cells, a series of CDs (APTMS, DAMO, AEEA, APTES, MAPS, or TPEDA) were synthesized by Ge et al. utilizing a one-step solvothermal process. In this study, APTMS CDs served as a representative. APTMS CDs were easy to prepare, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly, making them suited for mass production and upcoming biological applications. The developed APTMS CDs could quickly enter cells after being incubated with them and then instantaneously and steadily target mitochondria (with a mitochondrial imaging period of at least 24 h); this ensured the application of CDs for mitochondrial imaging. Moreover, the significant fluorescence contrast in mitochondria allowed APTMS CDs to be used to discriminate between malignant and healthy cells. We believe that this is the first time that CDs have been utilized to distinguish between malignant and healthy cells. The current work, in our opinion, expands the potential uses of CDs and could have repercussions for biomedical research areas that focus on mitochondria [95].

Some researchers looked at the CDs' metabolic process, tumor absorption mechanism, and in vivo biodistribution. According to Huang et al., the CDs could be quickly eliminated from the body in mice using different injection routes. The clearance rate was determined to be intravenous, intramuscular, and subcutaneous, [96], as shown in Fig. 3
A. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3 B, Wang et.al. [97], found that the polymer-coated nitrogen-doped CDs accumulated within the glioma based on the enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR) and were primarily administered to the liver, brain, and kidneys, 1.5 h after injection for glioma targeted imaging. When CDs were injected into mice, the cells in the tumor primarily and bladder absorbed the CDs; liver cells barely absorbed any CDs. Through radioelement labeling and positron emission tomography dynamic tests, Nadia et al. explored the uptake and biodistribution of CDs in rats and mice. Their findings revealed a quick renal clearance of CDs from the body. However, the zeta potential, surface charge, and hydrophilicity of CDs are strongly influencing the biodistribution and pharmacokinetic features of CDs [98] (Fig. 3 C). The studies mentioned above demonstrated a correlation between surface functional groups, zeta potential values, and the synthesis and purification processes of CDs and they are in vivo toxicity, absorption, biodistribution, and clearance. In conclusion, these studies offered encouraging data for researchers to examine fluorescent CD uses for drug delivery, therapeutic effect monitoring, and other applications. In the meantime, it is important to continue researching to modify their surfaces to lessen non-specific protein adsorption into CDs and to ensure quick transport to the targeted organ to reduce the cytotoxicity when used as imaging agents.

Since many years ago, chemotherapy with the active ingredients cisplatin and doxorubicin has been used to treat a variety of solid tumors, and both of them can affect DNA replication and prevent cancer cell division [99,100]. However, their poor targeting and toxic side effects on normal cells lead to chronic organ damage. To create a drug delivery system that precisely targets cancer cells, CDs with exceptional optical properties, biocompatibility, and sensitivity in FL imaging have been used as imaging-guided nanocarriers for labeling and monitoring chemotherapy drugs [101–103]. Zheng's team has created a medication delivery platform based on CD imaging guidance and Forster resonance energy transfer. This platform demonstrated real-time drug release monitoring, continuous fluorescence, convenient cell imaging, and great biocompatibility [104]. However, its sensitive surface characteristics to complex tumor microenvironments limited their efficacy and utilization in practice and in vivo. In response to the growing demand for specialized fluorescent bioimaging agents, Feng and colleagues created a CD-based cisplatin prodrug nanocarrier to achieve charge convertibility when the pH of the tumor extracellular changed, enabling imaging-guided drug delivery [105]. The charge-convertible CDs demonstrated high tumor growth suppression capabilities with few side effects. In addition, Gong et al. produced phosphorus and nitrogen-doped hollow CDs, a pH-dependent drug-releasing nanocarrier with a high drug loading, tiny particle size, rapid cellular absorption, hollow structure, and more hydroxyl/phosphate/pyridinic/pyrrolic-like functional groups [106].

Overcoming the nuclear barrier and endosomal/lysosomal entrapping and accomplishing the nucleus drug delivery and photodynamic therapy is still a significant hurdle for cancer therapy. Therefore, CD-based nuclear drug delivery devices have been created to treat tumors. To build real-time imaging drug delivery and photodynamic therapy nanostructures, Pan's team coupled FeN-CDs with FA, riboflavin (Rf), and DOX to create a GP-Rf-FA-FeN-CDs-DOX complex [107]. The outcomes demonstrated that the compound could effectively deliver DOX to targeted cells and release it intracellularly under NIR irradiation. These results aid in effective tumor eradication by chemo-photo synergistic therapy. The Zhang group utilized polyethylenimine caped CDs (P-CDs) and HA Conjugated-Dox to combine bioimaging and targeted drug delivery (HA-Dox). The findings demonstrated that the strong affinity of HA to CD44 receptors allowed the P-CDs/HA Dox to precisely target and penetrate cancer cells. Additionally, P-CDs/HA-Dox restored P-CDs' fluorescence when HAase was activated, resulting in enzyme-dependent Dox release for efficient cancer treatment [108]. The previous research demonstrates CDs' capacity as smart nanocarriers with improved chemotherapeutic effects and offers techniques to increase their future clinical applications in image-guided cancer theranostics. It is still essential to research the biological toxicity of these targeted drug delivery cargo based on CD imaging to normal tissues and the selective targeting and lengthening of the residency time in cancer tissues.

Biocompatible CDs, especially those used in optical cancer theranostic, have lot potentials to use as a nanoplatform for cancer imaging applications. Excitation and emission of CDs in both NIR-I and NIR-II windows are strongly interfered with by the spontaneous FL emission of biomolecules and the background light scattering of biological samples in the short-wavelength region, making it challenging to provide deeper FL penetration into tissue samples and achieve effective imaging contrast [109,110]. Ge and colleagues manufactured the first red emissive CDs with substantial photoacoustic (PA) and photothermal therapy (PTT) conversion efficiency. These CDs were utilized for intravenous imaging of living mice [111]. As a result of the EPR impact, the CDs accumulated in the tumor cells. After intravenous CD injections were administered to tumor-bearing nude mice, the tumor area showed a substantial FL signal compared to other tissues [112].

Ge et al. used poly thiophene benzoic acid to create red-emitting CDs. The CDs produced dual PDT and PTT effects with singlet oxygen (1O2) formation and strong PTT conversion efficiency under laser irradiation, emitting brilliant FL in the red region. The synthesized CDs act as theranostic platforms for imaging-guided PDT-PTT combination therapy in the therapeutic window, as shown in Fig. 4
A [5]. The red-shifted PL emission may also be produced by doping nitrogen atoms into CDs. These findings inspired Zheng and colleagues, who built multifunctional CDs to improve red region absorption and encourage an in vivo water splitting mechanism. The authors used the protoporphyrin photosensitizer, a tumor-targeting Arg-Gly-Asp, to create CDs with the polymer to overcome tumor hypoxia (PCCN). According to cell viability studies, the water splitting CDs had good growth inhibition of cancer cells and could suppress the hypoxia-triggered PDT resistance. As seen in Fig. 4 B, in vivo tests further proved PCCN's better capacity to combat tumor hypoxia. A hydrophobic cyanine dye and PEG were used to create novel NIR CDs (CyCD) by Zheng et al. These CDs demonstrated a preference for tumor cells and a high PTT conversion efficiency [113]. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the results showed that the synthesized CDs were an excellent option for cancer imaging and therapy. Ye's team doped F and N in CDs using a one-step microwave carbonation process, resulting in CDs with red wavelengths emissions, to further boost the level of redshift [114]. Additionally, Zhou's team developed chitosan CD hybrid nanogels bearing (CCHNs, 65 nm) using NIR imaging-guided cancer therapy [115]. The dual-responsive properties of CCHNs demonstrated high colloidal stability and bright and stable FL, efficient NIR PTT conversion, and intelligent drug release, as shown in Fig. 3D. Due to the confined surface plasmon, metal particles may also increase fluorescence intensity and NIR absorption qualities. For the use of cancer theranostic nanoplatforms, N-doped CDs and transition metal ions (Cu) were designed [116]. The findings demonstrated using Cu and N-CDs as thermal imaging agents to monitor the temperature dynamics during cancer photothermal/photodynamic therapy (PTT/PDT). These functional red emission CDs show potential for NIR fluorescent cancer imaging. They can also be used for imaging-guided PTT/PDT combo therapy in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as an imaging probe for brain tumor tissue
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the blood–brain barrier's blocking effect, one of the trickiest problems in neuroscience applications is getting fluorescent probes for both normal neuron terminals and brain tumors (BBB). CDs' small particle sizes and hydrophilic surface characteristics are crucial for their BBB penetration because they prolong circulation time and encourage passive targeting by increasing permeability and retention [117,118]. The therapy of malignant gliomas may now have new hope thanks to the discovery that CDs with small diameters (45 nm) had excellent BBB crossing skills and a high propensity for accumulating in the brain over other organs. The CDs could also target a glioma xenograft in mice's brains. Epifluorescence imaging showed that they reached their peak uptake in the brain just 5 min after being injected into the tail vein. The mice promptly removed the CDs from the tumor location. Then, using glucose and aspartic acid (Asp) were to target glioma model the most typical primary tumor. Zheng et al. created unique, tunable full-color emitting CDs with good biocompatibility [2]. The high-contrast biodistribution fluorescent signals of the Asp-functional CDs were detected in the glioma mass 20 min after tail vein injection, indicating that the ability of these CDs to freely penetrate the BBB and precisely target the glioma site rather than healthy brain tissues enables them to treat cancerous tumors successfully. Therefore, asp-CDs as an FL probe has the potential for guided targeting to diagnose glioma, one of the significant intracranial primary malignancies. Furthermore, Lu's study's in vitro BBB model and real-time live-cell imaging showed that the biocompatible N-CDs could cross the BBB [119]. Consequently, these PEI-passivated CDs with BBB-penetration ability show potential for delivering drugs to the brain. The idea of employing transferrin-conjugated CDs to transport medications over the BBB to target cells has been thoroughly investigated. CDs show significant BBB crossing abilities and a tendency to accumulate in brain tumors.

Due to their distinct physicochemical features, carbon-based nanomaterials are an emerging technology in biomedical applications. The creation of CDs is a simple operation that may be accomplished with readily available, inexpensive materials [120]. A standardized procedure for synthesizing CDs is still lacking, especially regarding good manufacturing practices with a known morphology, content, structure, and high quantum yield. This is necessary to commercialize the items for use in clinical applications and gain Food and Drug Administration regulatory approval for clinical trials. Future research will focus on synthesizing CDs with special targeting features, ideal forms, and sizes using changed surfaces and customized band gaps. Sentinel node localization illustrates how CDs are used in clinical cancer imaging (SLN). The SLN is where cancer is most likely to spread first. The sentinel node in breast cancer is typically found in the axillary nodes beneath the arm. The SLN biopsy is a surgical procedure to ascertain whether cancer has migrated from a primary tumor to your lymphatic system [121].

SLN mapping now uses organic dyes and radio colloids; however, doing so exposes patients to radiation that damages tissue. Although there have been some experimental and preclinical animal experiments, CDs are the ideal technology to be employed for live SLN mapping. However, we are still awaiting the transition to the clinical situation [122]. The CDs have better deep tissue penetration and are photostable. Studies on the effects of CDs on the BBB and sluggish removal of CDs have raised concerns about the potential buildup. However, a promising result using CDs in recent in vivo trials indicates enormous promise for cancer diagnosis and treatment. But compared to conventional QDs, CDs exhibit unique qualities that broaden their biological applications [109]. They are confronted with several difficulties, including biocompatibility, toxicity, a lifetime in use, pharmacokinetics, removal from the body, BBB, and in vivo targeting effectiveness. The latter is also influenced by the tissue's color and depth, which must both be imaged. Before beginning clinical trials in humans, it seems essential to expand research on CDs within the biological context of preclinical animal model studies [123]. The behavior of CDs in physiological and biological matrices should be indicated using novel techniques, although this step can also be quite difficult. Although there hasn't been much research done on the subject, CDs may be a good choice for the delivery of drugs and genes. Scientists are using targeted receptors to enhance the precise targeting of CDs in the transport of genes and drugs, although more study is still required [110,124]. Given the potential of surface-modified CDs, we anticipate that in the future, more research will be devoted to the advancement of modification techniques to get around CDs' limitations for biological applications as multifunctional agents, such as their simultaneous use as imaging and sensing probes and delivery agents. In conclusion, there is still much room for research and development of the ideal CDs, which is indicative of the use of CDs in biomedical applications in the future. This is due to the lack of optimal design for CDs and surface functionalization and conjugation to drugs and other molecules for delivery to target cells.

Fluorescent CDs, which have extraordinary imaging sensitivity, long-wavelength penetration, surface multi-functionality, and economical and environmentally acceptable synthesis processes, have made great strides in biomedical applications over the past decade. We've covered current breakthroughs in CD-based in vitro bioimaging, concentrating on cancer cells, stem cells, brain cells, the nucleus, and other cell organelles. We highlight recent studies, such as the production of unique CDs by modifying their surface and doping and their usage in vitro and in vivo imaging. Despite the rapid progress, more research is needed to completely understand CDs' primary function and long-term toxicity in therapeutic applications. First, CD modification techniques have been created to improve toxicity and targeting, which is critical for clinical diagnosis and treatment. Bioimaging of cancer with CD-based nano-probes requires more research. New therapy strategies that combine CDs with anticancer drugs and photodynamic and photothermal agents might improve cancer treatment. CDs coupled with novel functional groups or targeting molecules on their surface could help establish a multipurpose platform for targeting specific cell types and diagnosing and treating malignancies. CDs with NIR FL emissions or FLs with up-conversion and strong BBB piercing abilities are also needed. Despite numerous studies utilizing cell lines, mice, or zebrafish showing CDs' biocompatibility, a complete and systematic assessment of CDs' toxicity is required for clinical diagnosis and therapy. CD-based nano-probes for bioimaging of cancer need more study. New therapy strategies that combine CDs with anticancer drugs and photodynamic and photothermal agents might improve cancer treatment.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Abduladheem Turki Jalil received his B.Ec. degree in Biology from the University of Wasit: Al Kut, Wasit, Iraq, M.Sc. in Microbiology from the Yanka Kupala State University of Grodno: Hrodna, BY, and graduated in 2022 in Ph.D. of Virology from Yanka Kupala State University of Grodno, Belarus. He is currently Assistant professor in Medical Laboratories Techniques Department, Al-Mustaqbal University College, Babylon, Hilla, 51001, Iraq. His current research includes Cancer Research, Biomaterial Engineering, Dug Delivery and Theranostic Nanomaterials in the cancer field.
